title: Care Coordination & Safe Discharge Planning After Inpatient Psychiatric Hospitalization
subtitle: A 29-year-old man is ready for discharge 10 days after admission for major depression with a suicide attempt. He is stabilized on an SSRI, future-oriented, and denies suicidal ideation. The nurse must coordinate a safe transition of care and prevent the post-discharge risk spike.

patient.name: Daniel Okafor
patient.admitDate: Inpatient psychiatry day 10 after major depressive disorder with a suicide attempt. Stabilized on sertraline, engaged in care, future-oriented, denies current suicidal ideation, sleeping/eating better. Discharge planned today; outpatient follow-up and safety plan being finalized.

labs.v: Low today; no current ideation or plan.
Written plan complete; firearms removed; 988 provided.

nursesNote: Daniel Okafor, 29, inpatient psychiatry day 10 for MDD with a suicide attempt. Stabilized on sertraline, future-oriented, denies SI; PHQ-9 down 22→8. Discharge today. Coordinating medication reconciliation, a 7-day outpatient follow-up with warm hand-off, safety plan, lethal-means counseling, and community resources via social work/case management.
providerOrders: Discharge home on sertraline; reconcile and teach all medications
Schedule outpatient psychiatry follow-up within 7 days before discharge
Review safety plan, 988 crisis line, and lethal-means counseling with patient/family
Social work/case management for transport, housing, and insurance barriers
Complete warm hand-off to the outpatient provider and document the plan

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does a safe psychiatric discharge require?

opts.text: Confirm a written safety plan and the 988 crisis line are in place
Reconcile discharge medications and verify the patient understands them
Ensure an outpatient follow-up is scheduled before discharge
Reassess current suicide risk and access to lethal means
Discharge without follow-up is fine because he feels better today
Involve case management for transport, housing, and insurance barriers

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What indicates readiness and what drives post-discharge risk?

opts.text: A follow-up within ~7 days lowers readmission and post-discharge risk
The weeks AFTER psychiatric discharge are a high-risk window for suicide
Medication reconciliation prevents errors during the transition
Because he denies SI today, lethal-means counseling is unnecessary
Engaging support persons (with consent) improves adherence and safety
A warm hand-off to the outpatient provider improves continuity
Reassess current suicide risk and confirm clinical stability for discharge
Reconcile medications and complete teaching with teach-back
Confirm the outpatient follow-up and complete the warm hand-off
Review the safety plan, crisis line, and lethal-means counseling
Coordinate community resources and document the discharge plan

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Supports or undermines a safe transition?

opts.text: Schedule outpatient follow-up within 7 days before discharge
Use teach-back to confirm medication understanding
Provide the 988 crisis line and a written safety plan
Counsel on removing or securing lethal means at home
Coordinate transport, housing, and insurance via case management
Reconcile meds and send the list to the outpatient provider
Discharge without a confirmed follow-up appointment
Hand over prescriptions with no teaching
Skip lethal-means counseling because he denies SI today
Omit the warm hand-off and assume the patient will call

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence the discharge and warm hand-off
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify stability and review the finalized discharge plan with the patient
Perform medication reconciliation and teach-back
Confirm the follow-up appointment and complete the warm hand-off
Review the safety plan, crisis line, and lethal-means counseling with patient/family
Document the plan and provide written instructions and resources
The nurse confirmed a 7-day follow-up and completed a warm hand-off
Medication reconciliation with teach-back was completed
Safety plan, 988 line, and lethal-means counseling were reviewed
Family was engaged with the patient's consent
The discharge plan and instructions were documented
The patient was discharged with no follow-up because he felt better
Lethal-means counseling was skipped because he denied SI
Prescriptions were handed over without any teaching
No hand-off was made to the outpatient provider
